words.shortDefinition: A mathematical notation that describes the limiting behavior of a function when the argument tends towards a particular value or infinity.
words.simpleDefinition: A way to describe how much slower an algorithm gets when you feed it more data — like saying 'this takes twice as long for every extra thousand items.'
words.longDefinition: Used in computer science to classify algorithms according to how their run time or space requirements grow as the input size grows. It focuses on the 'worst-case' scenario.
words.techContext: We decided to switch from an O(n^2) nested loop to an O(n log n) sorting approach to handle the larger dataset.
words.exampleSentence: The search function has a Big O complexity of O(log n), making it extremely efficient for large arrays.
words.etymology: Named after the 'Order' of the function.
words.connector: Think of Big O like fuel efficiency ratings on cars — it tells you how the algorithm performs as the workload scales up, not the exact time it takes.

words.shortDefinition: An algorithmic technique for solving a complex problem by breaking it down into simpler subproblems.
words.longDefinition: It involves storing the results of subproblems to avoid redundant calculations (memoization or tabulation). It is typically used for optimization problems where subproblems overlap.
words.techContext: We used dynamic programming to find the shortest path in the grid, storing the cost to reach each cell in a 2D array.
words.exampleSentence: Dynamic programming turned our exponential-time recursive function into a linear-time solution.
words.etymology: Coined by Richard Bellman in the 1950s; 'programming' referred to planning/scheduling.

words.shortDefinition: An algorithm that makes the locally optimal choice at each stage with the hope of finding a global optimum.
words.longDefinition: Greedy algorithms are often simple and efficient but do not always produce the best possible solution. They work best for problems that exhibit the 'greedy choice property' and 'optimal substructure'.
words.techContext: We used a greedy algorithm for the coin change problem since our currency system allows for it.
words.exampleSentence: Dijkstra's algorithm is a famous greedy algorithm used to find the shortest path in a graph.
words.etymology: from 'greedy' (having an intense/selfish desire), reflecting the 'take the best now' approach.

words.shortDefinition: A method of solving a problem where the solution depends on solutions to smaller instances of the same problem.
words.simpleDefinition: A function that calls itself to solve smaller versions of the same problem until it hits a simple base case that can be solved directly.
words.longDefinition: In programming, recursion occurs when a function calls itself. Every recursive function must have a 'base case' to stop the execution and prevent an infinite loop (stack overflow).
words.techContext: I used recursion to traverse the folder structure, as each folder can contain more folders of the same type.
words.exampleSentence: The recursive solution for the Fibonacci sequence is elegant but highly inefficient without memoization.
words.connector: Think of recursion like Russian nesting dolls — each doll contains a smaller version of itself, until you reach the tiniest doll that can't be opened further.

words.shortDefinition: An algorithm design paradigm that recursively breaks down a problem into two or more sub-problems of the same or related type.
words.longDefinition: The sub-problems are solved independently, and their solutions are combined to give a solution to the original problem. Examples include Merge Sort, Quick Sort, and Binary Search.
words.techContext: Merge Sort uses a divide and conquer strategy to sort an array by splitting it in half until each piece has only one element.
words.exampleSentence: Divide and conquer algorithms are often easy to parallelize because the sub-problems don't share state.
words.etymology: Translation of the Latin maxim 'divide et impera'.

words.shortDefinition: A general algorithm for finding all (or some) solutions to some computational problems.
words.longDefinition: It incrementally builds candidates for the solutions and abandons a candidate ('backtracks') as soon as it determines that the candidate cannot possibly be completed to a valid solution.
words.techContext: We used backtracking to solve the Sudoku puzzle by trying numbers and reversing the choice if we hit a dead end.
words.exampleSentence: Backtracking is the standard approach for solving constraint satisfaction problems like the N-Queens problem.

words.shortDefinition: An optimization technique used primarily to speed up computer programs by storing the results of expensive function calls.
words.longDefinition: When the same inputs occur again, the function returns the cached result instead of recomputing it. This is a key part of the top-down approach in dynamic programming.
words.techContext: Adding memoization to our recursive Fibonacci function changed its time complexity from O(2^n) to O(n).
words.exampleSentence: I used a simple hash map for memoization to cache the API responses and avoid redundant network calls.

words.shortDefinition: A technique used to perform a required operation on a specific window size of a given array or string.
words.longDefinition: Instead of using nested loops to process subsets of an array, you maintain two pointers that define the 'window'. As you move the window, you only update the result based on the elements that entered and left the window.
words.techContext: I used a sliding window to find the longest substring without repeating characters in O(n) time.
words.exampleSentence: The sliding window pattern is highly effective for problems involving contiguous sequences in an array.
words.etymology: Metaphor for a window that slides across a larger surface.

words.shortDefinition: A technique where two pointers iterate through a data structure until one or both of them hit some condition.
words.longDefinition: Common variations include pointers moving at different speeds (slow and fast pointers for cycle detection) or pointers starting at opposite ends and moving toward each other (for palindrome checks or sorted sums).
words.techContext: We used the fast and slow pointer technique to detect a cycle in our linked list data structure.
words.exampleSentence: Using two pointers on a sorted array allowed us to find a pair that sums to a target value in linear time.

words.shortDefinition: Breadth-First Search; an algorithm for traversing or searching tree or graph data structures.
words.longDefinition: It starts at the tree root (or some arbitrary node of a graph) and explores all of the neighbor nodes at the present depth prior to moving on to the nodes at the next depth level. It uses a queue.
words.techContext: We used BFS to find the shortest path in an unweighted graph because it visits nodes in order of their distance from the start.
words.exampleSentence: A BFS traversal of the social network allowed us to find all 'Level 1' connections before looking at 'Level 2'.

words.shortDefinition: Depth-First Search; an algorithm for traversing or searching tree or graph data structures.
words.longDefinition: The algorithm starts at the root and explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. It uses a stack (often the call stack via recursion).
words.techContext: I used DFS to determine if there was a path between two cities in our transport network.
words.exampleSentence: DFS is typically used for topological sorting and finding strongly connected components in a graph.

words.shortDefinition: An algorithm for finding the shortest paths between nodes in a graph.
words.longDefinition: It finds the shortest path from a starting node to all other nodes in a weighted graph with non-negative edge weights. It uses a priority queue to always expand the 'cheapest' known node.
words.techContext: Our navigation engine uses Dijkstra's algorithm to calculate the quickest route between two addresses.
words.exampleSentence: Dijkstra's algorithm is much more efficient than BFS for graphs where the edges represent distances or costs.
words.etymology: Named after the Dutch computer scientist Edsger W. Dijkstra.

words.shortDefinition: An efficient algorithm for finding an item from a sorted list of items.
words.simpleDefinition: A fast way to find something in a sorted list by repeatedly cutting the search space in half — like opening a dictionary to the middle and deciding which half contains your word.
words.longDefinition: It works by repeatedly dividing in half the portion of the list that could contain the item, until you've narrowed down the possible locations to just one. It has a time complexity of O(log n).
words.techContext: Since our array was already sorted, we used binary search to find the user ID in just 10 comparisons instead of 1000.
words.exampleSentence: Binary search is the standard way to find an element in a large sorted dataset.
words.connector: Think of binary search like the number-guessing game 'higher or lower' — each guess cuts the possibilities in half until you find the answer.

words.shortDefinition: A highly efficient, in-place sorting algorithm.
words.longDefinition: A divide-and-conquer algorithm that works by selecting a 'pivot' element and partitioning the other elements into two sub-arrays, according to whether they are less than or greater than the pivot.
words.techContext: QuickSort is often faster in practice than Merge Sort, although its worst-case time complexity is O(n^2).
words.exampleSentence: Most standard libraries use a variation of QuickSort or Timsort for their built-in sort functions.

words.shortDefinition: The amount of time it takes for an algorithm to run, as a function of the length of the input.
words.simpleDefinition: A measure of how much longer an algorithm takes to run when you give it bigger inputs — focusing on the growth pattern, not exact milliseconds.
words.longDefinition: Quantifies the amount of time taken by an algorithm to run as a function of the size of the input to the problem. It is commonly expressed using Big O notation.
words.techContext: The time complexity of this nested loop is O(n^2), which is why it's timing out for large inputs.
words.exampleSentence: Understanding time complexity is vital for building applications that scale gracefully as user data grows.
words.connector: Think of time complexity like a recipe's difficulty rating — it tells you how much harder cooking gets when you double the guest count, not the exact minutes it takes.

words.shortDefinition: The amount of memory an algorithm uses, as a function of the length of the input.
words.simpleDefinition: A measure of how much extra memory an algorithm needs when you give it bigger inputs — counting both workspace and the input itself.
words.longDefinition: Quantifies the total amount of space (memory) needed by an algorithm to run to completion. This includes both the auxiliary space (extra space) and the space used by the input itself.
words.techContext: While the recursive solution is clean, its space complexity of O(n) on the stack is a concern for very deep trees.
words.exampleSentence: By using an in-place sorting algorithm, we reduced the space complexity of our data processing pipeline to O(1).
words.connector: Think of space complexity like packing for a trip — some packing methods need extra suitcases as the trip gets longer, others just need one bag regardless.

words.shortDefinition: A linear ordering of vertices in a directed acyclic graph (DAG).
words.longDefinition: In this ordering, for every directed edge uv from vertex u to vertex v, u comes before v. It is used for tasks with dependencies, such as scheduling jobs or resolving build dependencies.
words.techContext: I used a topological sort to determine the correct order to run our microservice migration scripts.
words.exampleSentence: The build failed because the topological sort detected a circular dependency between two modules.

words.shortDefinition: An informed search algorithm used in pathfinding and graph traversal.
words.longDefinition: It is an extension of Dijkstra's algorithm that uses a 'heuristic' function to estimate the remaining distance to the goal. This allows it to find the shortest path much faster by prioritizing nodes that seem to be closer to the target.
words.techContext: The enemy AI in our game uses A* to find the quickest path to the player while avoiding obstacles.
words.exampleSentence: A* search is the standard choice for pathfinding in 2D and 3D space due to its performance and optimality.
words.etymology: Named as an 'A' algorithm with an improved evaluation function.

words.shortDefinition: An algorithm that computes shortest paths from a single source vertex to all of the other vertices in a weighted graph.
words.longDefinition: Unlike Dijkstra's, Bellman-Ford can handle graphs with negative edge weights. It is also used to detect 'negative cycles' in a graph.
words.techContext: We used Bellman-Ford to find arbitrage opportunities in the currency exchange graph, where a negative cycle represents a profit.
words.exampleSentence: Bellman-Ford is slower than Dijkstra's, with a time complexity of O(V*E), but its ability to handle negative weights is crucial for certain financial applications.
words.etymology: Named after Richard Bellman and Lester Ford Jr.

words.shortDefinition: The act of algorithmically manipulating bits or other pieces of data shorter than a word.
words.longDefinition: Uses bitwise operators like AND, OR, XOR, and NOT to perform tasks extremely efficiently. It's used in low-level systems programming, cryptography, and image processing.
words.techContext: I used bit manipulation to pack multiple boolean flags into a single integer, saving space in our database.
words.exampleSentence: Understanding bit manipulation is essential for optimizing performance-critical code in embedded systems.

codeExample.code:
// Check if n is even using bitwise AND
const isEven = (n) => (n & 1) === 0;

words.shortDefinition: A classic problem in combinatorial optimization.
words.longDefinition: Given a set of items, each with a weight and a value, determine the number of each item to include in a collection so that the total weight is less than or equal to a given limit and the total value is as large as possible.
words.techContext: We modeled our server resource allocation as a variation of the knapsack problem to maximize the number of tasks we can run on a single instance.
words.exampleSentence: The 0/1 knapsack problem is a famous NP-complete problem that is often solved using dynamic programming.
words.etymology: Metaphor for filling a 'knapsack' (backpack) with the most valuable items.

words.shortDefinition: An algorithm for finding shortest paths in a weighted graph with positive or negative edge weights (but no negative cycles).
words.longDefinition: Unlike Dijkstra's (which is single-source), Floyd-Warshall finds the shortest paths between *all* pairs of vertices in a graph in a single execution. It has a time complexity of O(V^3).
words.techContext: We used the Floyd-Warshall algorithm to pre-calculate the distances between all cities in our delivery network.
words.exampleSentence: Floyd-Warshall is simple to implement using three nested loops and a 2D distance matrix.
words.etymology: Named after Robert Floyd and Stephen Warshall.

words.shortDefinition: A set of decision problems to which any problem in NP can be reduced in polynomial time.
words.longDefinition: A problem is NP-complete if it is in NP (its solutions can be verified quickly) and it is as hard as the hardest problems in NP. No efficient (polynomial time) algorithm is known to exist for any NP-complete problem.
words.techContext: Since the task scheduling problem is NP-complete, we used a greedy heuristic instead of trying to find the absolute optimal solution.
words.exampleSentence: Proving that a problem is NP-complete tells us that we should probably look for an approximation algorithm rather than an exact one.

words.shortDefinition: A stable, divide-and-conquer sorting algorithm.
words.longDefinition: It works by recursively splitting an array in half, sorting the halves, and then merging the sorted halves back together. It has a guaranteed time complexity of O(n log n) and is excellent for sorting linked lists.
words.techContext: We chose Merge Sort over QuickSort for this task because we needed a stable sort that preserves the relative order of equal elements.
words.exampleSentence: Merge Sort requires O(n) extra space for the temporary arrays used during the merge step.

words.shortDefinition: A bottom-up approach to dynamic programming.
words.longDefinition: Unlike memoization (top-down), tabulation involves solving all subproblems starting from the smallest ones and filling up a table (usually an array). This avoids the overhead of recursion and potential stack overflows.
words.techContext: We implemented the knapsack problem using tabulation to ensure our memory usage was predictable and the execution was fast.
words.exampleSentence: The tabulation approach to dynamic programming is usually more efficient than memoization for problems where almost all subproblems need to be solved.
